methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: Collaborative Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Fairmat
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $350M

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Tritone Technologies is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company founded by Ofer Ben-Zur. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of six same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing, supplemented by one public reference point and eight additional private companies. The primary peer set spans valuations from $300M to $10.0B.

Peer-median analysis is the applicable triangulation method here. The median valuation across the six direct comparables—including 1X Technologies at $10.0B, Bedrock Robotics at $1.8B, Dexterity at $1.6B, Collaborative Robotics at $500M, and Fairmat at $350M—yields $1.1B. This method anchors the valuation given the availability of contemporaneous Series B data points within the target sector and stage.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $914M to $1.2B, centered on the peer median of $1.1B. The wide dispersion within the comp set ($300M to $10.0B) reflects meaningful heterogeneity in robotics company scale and market positioning. Key uncertainties include Tritone's relative progress on product commercialization, capital efficiency relative to peers, and addressable market focus within the broad manufacturing robotics landscape. Without additional triangulation methods (revenue multiples, DCF, or transaction precedents), confidence in precise valuation placement within the range remains limited. Investors should weight company-specific metrics—unit economics, customer concentration, technical differentiation—heavily in final pricing decisions.
